modules.module: M1: Why Most Side Projects Die and How Agile Fixes That

Claude.notes: Marcus's stalled extension creates the exact 'premature scaling / deferred decisions' problem the concept section answers. Each lesson opens with a specific dated vignette; characters change every lesson rather than recurring, which weakens character continuity but works as deliberate vignette design at this level. Quiz items are uniformly application-style.
Claude.topIssue: Lab 1 ('Sprint Autopsy') is an AI-chat conversation with no tangible deliverable — the learner leaves with insight rather than a written sprint plan they can submit or audit later.

Gemini.notes: Agile Manifesto framing (2001, four preferences, contrast with Waterfall) is technically accurate. Definitions of sprint, backlog, and velocity are correct for the solo-builder adaptation. The Napster/Fanning anecdote is approximately right but compresses a more complex history (Napster shipped June 1999 after months of work; the 'nine months' figure is defensible but uncited).
Gemini.topIssue: Several real-world claims (Napster's nine-month timeline; 'tools have compressed gap from months to days in 2024–2025') are stated as fact without sourcing — defensible but reads as confident assertion rather than grounded data.

Perplexity.notes: Tool references (Cursor, Replit, Lovable, Claude, Bolt.new) are current as of late 2024/2025 and represent the actual tools a 2026 solo builder is using. The 'speed-trap' framing maps well onto the current reality of vibe-coding tools. Story timeframes (March 2024) are recent enough to feel contemporaneous.
Perplexity.topIssue: Lab depends on a working AI-chat platform integration; if the embedded chat doesn't actually push back on vague answers as the lab promises, the lab's pedagogical value collapses to a generic prompt.

Claude.notes: Strong four-vignette structure — Marcus's vagueness, Priya/Devon's customer specificity, Jasmine's symptom vs. root, Theo's first-transaction MVP — each story creates the precise problem its lesson resolves. The 'Customer Specificity Ladder' and 'Could I Call Them' test are excellent insight-landing devices.
Claude.topIssue: Five Whys is attributed broadly to 'manufacturing quality-control' but not specifically to Sakichi Toyoda / Toyota Production System; minor sourcing weakness in an otherwise sharp concept section.

Gemini.notes: Customer Specificity Ladder and Five Whys mappings are conceptually sound. The MVP definition ('smallest possible thing that actually delivers core value to a real user') is correct in spirit but slightly elides the original Eric Ries formulation, which centers validated learning rather than direct value delivery.
Gemini.topIssue: Five Whys origin is described as 'manufacturing quality-control' without crediting Toyota/Sakichi Toyoda; MVP definition under-references Eric Ries's original learning-centric framing.

Perplexity.notes: Customer-specificity examples (dining-hall budgets, financial-aid appeals for first-gen students) read as authentic and current. The 'AI generates 50 features in 3 minutes' caution maps directly onto how Claude/ChatGPT behave today.
Perplexity.topIssue: Lab deliverable is again conversational; for currency, an artifact like a written user-story doc would survive the session better than chat ephemera.

modules.module: M3: Sprint Cycles for Solo Builders

Claude.notes: Vertical-slicing introduction is well-grounded. Four-vignette structure continues consistently. Concept density is heavier than M1–M2 — the module covers vertical slicing, the 4-question scope filter, AI-as-uncertainty, sprint reviews, pivots, and roadmaps in a single unit.
Claude.topIssue: Slight density imbalance: Lessons 3–4 cover sprint review + roadmap + pivot recognition + 'graduating from sprints' — risks attention fatigue without a corresponding lab artifact to anchor the second half.

Gemini.notes: Vertical-slicing concept is correctly distinguished from horizontal builds. Sprint-review framing is consistent with established Scrum practice. 'Spike sprint' usage is technically accurate.
Gemini.topIssue: The 'horizon planning' framing is a custom synthesis rather than a citable Agile framework — not wrong, but presented with more authority than the source material warrants.

Perplexity.notes: AI-as-uncertainty section reflects genuine 2024–2025 reality — model behavior is itself a project risk. Story timestamps (Jan 2024, March 2024, October 2023, May 2024) are recent.
Perplexity.topIssue: October 2023 story is now ~2.5 years old at scoring time; the AI-tool landscape has shifted enough that the framing ('what tools to test') would benefit from a refresh in a future revision.

modules.module: M4: AI Tools for Every Stage

Claude.notes: The four-stage research/build/test/iterate stack is a clean organizing frame. The 'three-question filter before accepting any AI-suggested feature' lands the central insight well. 'When AI Makes You a Worse Developer' is an honest counterweight that prevents the module from sliding into tool advocacy.
Claude.topIssue: The module is the most tool-name-dependent of the eight; specific tools cited (and their listed capabilities) will date faster than the rest of the course.

Gemini.notes: Tool capability descriptions are mostly accurate but compressed — e.g., 'AI-assisted QA without a testing team' is a strong claim that papers over real limitations of current LLM-driven test generation (flaky tests, weak edge-case coverage).
Gemini.topIssue: Several capability claims about AI testing tools are presented more confidently than the underlying technology supports today; learners may overestimate what AI-generated test suites actually catch.

Perplexity.notes: Tool grid (Cursor, Replit Agent, Lovable, Bolt.new, Claude) is accurate as of 2024–2025. Persist/Pivot/Quit framework is an evergreen contribution that won't date.
Perplexity.topIssue: The specific AI tool names are likely to require refresh within 12 months as the agentic-IDE category consolidates; module would benefit from a maintenance note about which categories matter rather than which products.

modules.module: M5: Handling Scope Creep When AI Makes Everything Feel Possible

Claude.notes: The 'Yes Machine' framing is among the strongest narrative devices in the course — it creates the problem the entire module resolves and stays in the learner's vocabulary. MVB (Minimum Viable Boundary) is a useful neologism that stays grounded.
Claude.topIssue: Lab 4 ('Triage Session') would benefit from a written deliverable — a feature list with explicit cut/keep decisions — rather than another conversational chat.

Gemini.notes: Definition-of-Done framing is technically consistent with Scrum. The 'dopamine loop' framing is psychologically loose — a useful metaphor but not a clinical claim.
Gemini.topIssue: The dopamine-loop section asserts neurochemical mechanisms without citation; reads compelling but is closer to popular psychology than established literature.

Perplexity.notes: AI-accelerated scope creep is the most current concern in 2024–2026 solo-builder discourse — this module captures it sharply. 'Three types of scope creep AI accelerates' is the highest-leverage section in the course.
Perplexity.topIssue: Reference to specific tool behaviors ('AI suggests an animation library when you ask about a button') will stay accurate longer than tool names but is still a near-term observation.

Claude.notes: 'Politeness loop' and 'future-tense endorsement' are precise diagnostic terms learners can use immediately. Cold-outreach guidance is unusually candid — most courses of this type sanitize the awkwardness of asking strangers to talk; this one names it.
Claude.topIssue: Iteration vs. pivot section is conceptually right but compressed; the 'how to pivot without starting from zero' guidance could be expanded into its own lesson.

Gemini.notes: Behavioral-validation framing aligns with Customer Development methodology (Steve Blank lineage) without explicit attribution. Three types of false-positive feedback are a useful taxonomy.
Gemini.topIssue: Customer-Development lineage is implicit; learners would benefit from knowing this approach has a 20-year intellectual history (Blank, Cooper) rather than feeling like course-original frameworks.

Perplexity.notes: Cold-outreach guidance is timeless and tracks how 2025 indie hackers actually find users (Reddit, niche Discord, X). Lab framing is executable today with any LLM-backed chat.
Perplexity.topIssue: Module doesn't cite specific 2024–2025 platforms where solo builders find first users (e.g., Indie Hackers, Reddit subs, niche Discords) — the practical 'where to find them' guidance is platform-agnostic when it could be more concrete.

modules.module: M7: Turning a Side Project into a Portfolio Piece or Income Stream

Claude.notes: Lower narrative density than earlier modules — appropriate as the course shifts toward applied output. Decision Log concept is useful and rare in builder pedagogy. 'So What?' test for portfolio framing is a real diagnostic learners can apply.
Claude.topIssue: Story-to-concept ratio drops noticeably here vs. M1–M6; the lessons feel more like advice than narrated discovery, which is acceptable for a portfolio module but reads as a tonal shift.

Gemini.notes: Pricing psychology section uses defensible heuristics ($10/$30/$100 anchors) but doesn't cite the underlying behavioral pricing research. AI cost-management guidance is technically reasonable.
Gemini.topIssue: Pricing-psychology and 'first dollar' claims are presented without referencing established pricing research (Anchoring, Decoy Effect literature).

Perplexity.notes: AI-cost section reflects current API pricing reality (token costs, model selection trade-offs). 'First dollar' guidance is appropriate for 2025–2026 indie scale.
Perplexity.topIssue: Specific platform recommendations for portfolios (the 'where to put your work' section) would date faster than the principles; current platforms like Notion, GitHub Pages, custom domains stay valid but specific tool capabilities shift.

Claude.notes: Capstone module — synthesizes the whole course into a 30-day commitment. The four-week structure (week 1 setup → week 2 build → week 3 wall → week 4 launch) is an honest model of what actually happens. 'The Week 3 Wall' framing is the most useful single insight in the course.
Claude.topIssue: Lab 4 ('Launch Decision + Retrospective Draft') asks for a real artifact (retrospective draft) — strongest deliverable in the course, but still entirely AI-mediated rather than instructor-reviewed.

Gemini.notes: 85% Rule and Three Stuck Types (Technical/Domain/Motivation) are accurate diagnostic taxonomies. Sprint retrospective format aligns with established Scrum retrospective practice.
Gemini.topIssue: The '85% Rule' is presented as a definite principle but is closer to a heuristic — useful framing, but the precise threshold should be presented as a guideline rather than a rule.

Perplexity.notes: The 30-day-sprint framing matches how 2024–2026 indie builders actually structure first projects (build-in-public on X, Indie Hackers cohorts). Capability delta is unusually clear: 'You will have shipped a thing in 30 days.'
Perplexity.topIssue: Module doesn't reference current build-in-public communities (X/Indie Hackers/Reddit) where 30-day sprints are publicly tracked — a missed opportunity to anchor the structure in observable real-world practice.
